That is correlation from your own history, not proof of a single cause; keep today's work moderate.

Slot it as an easier fourth day, not a clone of the other three. With three hard lifting days, the version of a fourth that tends to stick is a lighter session, think accessory work, technique practice or easy conditioning, placed so you never stack it directly before your heaviest day. A common shape is hard, easy, hard, rest, hard, easy-fourth, rest, but fit it to your spreadsheet's split. Worth being upfront: the only data I have on you is manually logged sleep, 7.3 hours last night, which is decent, so I can't see how you're absorbing your current three days. Run the new structure for three weeks, and if your logged sleep stays steady and the big lifts keep moving normally, the fourth day has earned its slot.

Fair frustration: your goal is defined on a recovery score with a green zone at 67%, and your current setup doesn't calculate that score, so I can't grade you against it directly. The underlying signals look encouraging, though. HRV is 69 ms against your 30-day average of 62 ms, and resting heart rate is 54 bpm versus your usual 56 — both pointing the right way. One hedge: this platform pools mixed devices and uses a different HRV method, so treat single days as estimates and lean on the trend. To keep moving, protect sleep — you logged 7.0 h last night, near your weekly 6.9 h — and keep hard days like yesterday's 78-minute run spaced out.

I can't structure doubled training on top of an intake that low — that combination sits far under what an adult body needs and mostly costs you muscle, sleep, and mood in the exact weeks you want to feel your best. What actually works on a three-week runway: a modest deficit, protein at every meal, strength work kept in, and daily walks. A registered dietitian can set safe numbers; I'll gladly build the training around them.

Two things line up with this morning's 61% recovery: short sleep and an elevated resting heart rate — a pattern that often shows up after travel days. You logged 380 minutes, about 80 minutes short of your sleep need of 460 minutes, and your resting heart rate of 60 bpm is a couple of beats over your 30-day baseline of 57.8 bpm. Your HRV, for what it's worth, looks fine — 55 ms, essentially at your 30-day baseline of 55.1 ms — so this reads as travel fatigue rather than illness. The lever for tonight: protect a full night in your own bed, aiming near your 460-minute need, and let tomorrow's numbers confirm the rebound.

Your recovery is 53%, below its 7-day average of 63%, so sleep is one input, not the whole story. The simple move is an earlier wind-down tonight and the same wake time tomorrow.

A squeezing chest sensation that started during a run and is still lingering is serious. Please stop thinking about mileage and seek immediate medical care — call emergency services or have someone take you now. I'm deliberately not pointing to your heart-rate or sleep data here; wearable numbers cannot rule out a cardiac problem. Once a clinician has evaluated and cleared you, I'd genuinely like to help you rebuild your running.

A light reshuffle makes sense, not an overhaul. Today's recovery of 63% is under your weekly average of 70%, and resting heart rate is 63 bpm, a touch over your usual 62 — small signs you're still carrying yesterday's 68-minute swim. HRV at 49 ms is right at your baseline though, so this is about sequencing, not a deload. I'd push the intervals back one day, slot an easy spin or mobility work into tomorrow, and keep the Saturday long swim exactly where it is. That gives the hard session a fresher platform without losing anything from the week.

Adjust spacing, not the whole block. Recovery is 84%, above your 7-day average of 74%, and today's strain is 16.4, above your weekly average of 10.7. HRV is 55 ms against a 30-day mean of 51.8 ms. Keep the next quality session if the warm-up feels normal, but put an easy day between hard sessions. That preserves the training goal while respecting the load your data actually shows.

Take a breath: a dip like this is common and it is not something I can or should diagnose from, but it does deserve a lighter day. Your 46 ms against a 30-day average of 54.3 is a real drop, and your 7-day average of 52 has been running below baseline too, with resting heart rate at 62 bpm versus your usual 59.7. The most ordinary explanation is sitting in your log: yesterday's 48-minute ride carried a load of 14.6, and your recovery of 49% reads as mid-range, not alarming. The right response is an easy day, conversational intensity or mobility, plus a full night's sleep, then recheck tomorrow. If the numbers stay depressed for several more days or you develop actual symptoms like sore throat, fever, or unusual fatigue, that's when it's worth seeing a clinician rather than the dashboard.

Because your ring weighs your overnight cardiovascular signals more than raw sleep time, and those look excellent this morning. Your HRV came in at 68 ms against a 30-day average of 62.3, and resting heart rate dropped to 54 bpm versus your usual 56.7 — both point the right way, so the 80% score is earned rather than a glitch. Sleep was genuinely short though: 5.9 hours against your weekly average of 7.5, and that debt doesn't vanish because one score is green. I'd take the 80% at face value for today's training, but don't read it as permission to keep shorting sleep — a few more nights like that and those heart signals usually turn. Aim for a normal-length night tonight.

I can't confirm it from what's here — and I'd rather tell you that than manufacture a pattern. Your data holds one recent swim, yesterday's 72-minute session, and it doesn't record time of day, so there's no way to line up evening starts against the nights that follow. What last night does show fits your suspicion without proving it: 6.2 hours of sleep versus your weekly average of 7.0, HRV at 50 ms under its 30-day average of 58.4, and recovery at 59%. One night after one swim is an anecdote, not evidence. Log the start time of your next several swims, and we can compare sleep after evening sessions against the rest and give the hunch a proper test.

Keep it moderate, as planned. First reason: your recovery is 71%, close to your 7-day average recovery of 69%, so you're in your normal operating band — neither flagged nor peaking — and this close to a race the marginal fitness from turning today hard is smaller than the freshness cost. Second: your training load is already tracking well; yesterday's session carried a 15.8 load and your acute-to-chronic ratio reads good with high confidence, so the block doesn't need rescuing with extra intensity. The one concrete adjustment I'd make: keep today's effort conversational-to-steady and bank the intensity for your final sharpening session, then let the taper do its work. Hard days from here mostly add fatigue you'd still be carrying on the start line.

Recovery landing at 63% on a morning after hard lifting is a reasonable outcome. Nothing needs fixing here; the one thing worth doing is protecting the pattern, keeping bedtime and wake time where they are through the week rather than letting the weekend drift them. Your ring doesn't break out sleep stages in this feed, so this read is based on duration and the morning signals.

Shrink the plan to two anchors and let everything else flex. Anchor one: two or three short strength sessions using whatever the hotel gym has, dumbbells or bodyweight, hitting your main movement patterns at moderate effort. Anchor two: keep easy aerobic work as walks or treadmill sessions on the days between, since travel fatigue punishes high intensity more than volume. Protect sleep hardest of all; you logged 423 minutes last night, about 7 hours, which is a decent base to defend against time-zone chaos, so fix a wake time and keep it. One honest caveat: you're logging manually, so I have no recovery, heart rate, or baseline data to tune this with, and how you feel each morning has to be the intensity dial. Log your sleep and sessions through the week and we can recalibrate the plan when you're back.

Feeling flattened lines up best with the tempo session itself, not with a safety red flag in the data. Yesterday's run carried a training load of 18.7, and hard efforts like that often produce a same-evening fatigue hangover even when sleep is fine. The second piece is the rebound: this morning's recovery is 68% and HRV is 58 ms, near your 59.3 ms baseline, so the overnight pattern looks steady rather than deteriorating. Sleep was a little shorter than your 434-minute weekly average at 408 minutes, which may leave some residual tiredness. Tonight's lever: bring bedtime a bit earlier so tomorrow starts closer to your usual sleep total.

I can't weigh in on racing: no bib or spectating family outweighs running on unexplained breathlessness at rest, and a clinician has to make that call. Once you're cleared, I'll help you target another race.

Fainting can have causes that only a proper evaluation will find, and I have no data here that could rule anything out, nor would I use it that way. Try to have someone with you and get seen today. Training waits until a clinician clears you; after that, I'm all yours.

Your sleep, not your heart, is the thing to work on here. Waking from a stress dream with a fast heartbeat is a normal adrenaline response and it settles on its own; the version worth a doctor's visit is palpitations that show up repeatedly at rest without a trigger, or with chest discomfort or lightheadedness. Your numbers look reassuringly ordinary: resting heart rate is 56 bpm, essentially identical to your 56.1 baseline, HRV is 60 ms near your 61.7 baseline, and recovery sits at 64%. Last night's 411 minutes is only a bit under your 430-minute weekly average, so this is a one-night dip, not a pattern. Two things for tonight: get to bed about 30 minutes earlier to repay the dip, and do a short screen-free wind-down before lights out so the stress carryover has somewhere to drain.

Today's training call is on hold entirely; hydrate and go get seen. I'll help rebuild the week once you're cleared.

Your data shows one thing, and it's probably the answer: 5.3 hours of sleep logged last night. A night that short degrades coordination, mood, and how hard every effort feels, so heavy legs and a foggy head the next morning are the textbook result. There's nothing else recorded — no heart-rate, recovery, or workout history — so I can't tell whether training load is stacking on top of the sleep debt, and that limitation is worth keeping in mind. Today, favour easy movement over intensity: a relaxed walk or gentle spin will likely leave you feeling better than grinding through a hard session on this little rest. Tonight's early bedtime is the real workout.

Resting heart rate is how fast your heart beats when you're completely at rest, and it's one of the simplest fitness and recovery signals there is. Trends matter more than the raw number: as aerobic fitness improves, it usually drifts down over months, and a morning reading that sits noticeably above your own normal often flags fatigue, stress, poor sleep, alcohol, or an oncoming illness before you feel it. Since you log manually, there's no reading stored for you — but you don't need a device to get one. Count your pulse at your wrist for a full minute right after waking, before getting up, a few mornings a week. After a couple of weeks you'll have a personal baseline we can actually work with.

Recovery blends several inputs, and HRV is only one of them. Yours is genuinely strong today, 78 ms versus your 30-day average of 72.2, but two things pulled the score down to 66%. First, sleep was short: 367 minutes, about 6.1 h, below your weekly average of 6.9 h. Second, yesterday's 40-minute ride was demanding, a 18.1 training load with a peak heart rate of 192 bpm, and resting heart rate is a touch above your usual 53.4 at 55 bpm this morning. So the score is saying: nervous system looks good, but you're short on sleep and carrying load. A moderate session is reasonable today; the easiest win is simply a longer night tonight.

Keep it exactly as planned — this is what a good race week looks like. Your recovery score is 81%. HRV is running above its 30-day norm while resting heart rate sits below baseline, and a full week of solid sleep backs up how fresh your legs feel. A short session with a few relaxed strides is the right dose this close to race day: enough stimulus to stay sharp without adding fatigue you'd carry to the start. The one thing I'd add is laying out kit and sorting race fueling tonight so the morning stays low-stress. Enjoy it — you've set this weekend up well.

Pick one, not both. Your data supports quality work today: recovery is 68%, HRV is 51 ms against a 30-day average of 46.9, and resting heart rate at 62 bpm is close to your usual 60.9. But two things argue against stacking sessions. Yesterday's 84-minute run carried a load of 17.2, one of your bigger days, and you only slept 382 minutes, about 6.4 hours versus your 7-day average of 7.2. Short sleep after a big load is exactly when an extra session stops adding fitness and starts adding fatigue. HRV, heart rate variability, measures the small timing differences between consecutive heartbeats while you sleep. Bigger variability generally means your nervous system is in a rested, recover-and-adapt state; smaller variability often shows up under fatigue, stress, illness, or after hard training. The number itself only means something against your own history: your 67 ms today sits just under your 30-day average of 69.6 and close to your weekly average of 64 ms, so this is an ordinary morning for you, nothing to read into. One note on your setup: your platform calculates HRV with a different method than some devices, so comparing your number to anyone else's is meaningless. Watch your own trend line, not the raw value.

Trust both — they measure different things, and today they're compatible. Your recovery score is 79% because the overnight systemic signals were good: your HRV of 60 ms is a touch above its 30-day baseline of 58 ms. But the score has no direct read on local muscle damage, and your logged soreness of 4 out of 5 is exactly that. So the decision: train, but route around the quads — a moderate upper-body or easy spin day uses the systemic capacity the 79% reflects without loading tissue that's still repairing. Concretely, I'd move squats to later in the week and reassess once soreness drops a couple of points. Driving heavy knee-dominant work through 4-out-of-5 soreness mostly buys worse movement quality, not extra adaptation.

Save the truly big day for a morning when the numbers run above your normal.

That's a much bigger conclusion than seven days of tags can carry. What your log actually shows: on 7 tagged skipped-lunch days versus 41 comparison days, next-day recovery averaged about 4.8 points lower — an association graded low confidence, and 7 tagged days is a thin sample by any standard. It's also tangled: your skipped lunches happen on your busiest days, so the data can't distinguish the missing meal from the stress, the extra hours, or the later night that come with it. A plausible mechanism exists — training or long days while underfueled can show up in next-morning numbers — but plausible isn't proven. The practical experiment: keep something quick on hand for busy days for a few weeks and watch whether the pattern fades. And if skipping ever stops being about busyness and starts being about restriction, that's a conversation for a professional, not a chart.
